questions.stem: A child with fever is breathing faster than usual and has nasal flaring. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Reassess respiratory status and oxygenation immediately
Tell the parent the child is probably just warm
Wait until the next temperature check
Focus only on giving antipyretics

questions.rationale: Nasal flaring and tachypnea suggest respiratory distress and require prompt assessment. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Fever does not explain away respiratory distress. - C Delay is unsafe. - D Lowering the fever does not replace breathing assessment.

distractorRationales.b: "Tell the parent the child is probably just warm" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "**Why the others are wrong:** - B Fever does not explain away respiratory distress" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Wait until the next temperature check" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Reassess respiratory status and oxygenation immediately" fits the safer response to nasal flaring and tachypnea suggest respiratory distress and require prompt assessment.

questions.takeaway: Nasal flaring is a pediatric respiratory warning sign.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly has facial droop and one-sided arm weakness. What is the nurse's priority?

options.text: Activate the stroke response process immediately
Recheck in an hour to see whether it improves
Ask the patient to rest and avoid talking
Give pain medication first

questions.rationale: Sudden focal deficits can signal stroke, and rapid response matters because time-sensitive treatment may be available. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Waiting can cost treatment time. - C Rest does not address the emergency. - D Pain control is not the first priority here.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Activate the stroke response process immediately" fits the safer response to a patient suddenly has facial droop and one-sided arm weakness.
distractorRationales.c: "Ask the patient to rest and avoid talking" misses the bedside risk because - D Pain control is not the first priority here points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "Give pain medication first" moves care toward routine care before neurologic safety assessment.

questions.takeaway: Sudden one-sided weakness is a stroke emergency until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A nurse receives report on four patients. Which patient is UNSTABLE and requires immediate reassessment?

options.text: Post-op appendectomy patient, day 1, HR 92, BP 118/74, mild incisional pain 4/10
Patient with pneumonia, T 38.4°C, HR 88, RR 20, SpO₂ 95% on 3L NC, improving on antibiotics
Patient with GI bleed admitted 6 hours ago: HR 118 (was 88 one hour ago), BP 96/58 (was 118/72), increased bright red rectal bleeding
Patient with COPD exacerbation, SpO₂ 91% on 2L NC (baseline 90–92%), pursed-lip breathing

questions.rationale: The GI bleed patient shows a deteriorating trend over one hour: HR increased 30 bpm (compensatory tachycardia from volume loss), BP dropped 22/14 mmHg (hypoperfusion), and bleeding is visibly worsening. This trajectory — not just the absolute values — defines instability. A patient is unstable when vital signs are trending in the wrong direction, indicating the body's compensatory mechanisms are being overwhelmed. The post-op patient (A) is stable and expected. The pneumonia patient (B) is improving on treatment. The COPD patient (D) is at their baseline SpO₂.

distractorRationales.a: "Post-op appendectomy patient, day 1, HR 92, BP 118/74, mild incisional pain 4/10" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "The GI bleed patient shows a deteriorating trend over one hour: HR increased 30 bpm (compensatory tachycardia from" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before reassurance.
distractorRationales.b: "Patient with pneumonia, T 38.4°C, HR 88, RR 20, SpO₂ 95% on 3L NC, improving on antibiotics" misses the bedside risk because the GI bleed patient shows a deteriorating trend over one hour: HR increased 30 bpm (compensatory tachycardia from points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: Because the GI bleed patient shows a deteriorating trend over one hour: HR increased 30 bpm (compensatory tachycardia from suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, "Patient with COPD exacerbation, SpO₂ 91% on 2L NC (baseline 90–92%), pursed-lip breathing" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Instability = trending in the wrong direction. HR rising + BP falling + worsening bleeding = active hemorrhagic shock. Always compare current vitals to the baseline/trend, not just absolute values.

questions.stem: A patient with asthma is wheezing loudly, using accessory muscles, and can only say a few words at a time. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The patient is having significant respiratory distress
The wheezing proves the airway is opening well
The patient should rest without reassessment
Talking less always means improvement

questions.rationale: Accessory muscle use and limited speech suggest increased work of breathing and a worsening respiratory pattern. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Loud wheezing does not mean the patient is stable. - C Rest does not replace reassessment. - D Short speech can indicate more, not less, distress.

distractorRationales.b: "The wheezing proves the airway is opening well" misses the bedside risk because speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: "The patient should rest without reassessment" misses the bedside risk because speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "The patient is having significant respiratory distress" fits the safer response to a patient with asthma is wheezing loudly, using accessory muscles.

questions.takeaway: Speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: The nurse has four patients. Which should be seen first?

options.text: A patient with shortness of breath and accessory muscle use
A patient requesting help to the bathroom
A patient asking for a TV remote
A patient waiting for routine teaching

questions.rationale: Breathing difficulty with accessory muscle use is an immediate assessment priority. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Bathroom help is important but less urgent. - C The remote is nonurgent. - D Routine teaching can wait.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "A patient with shortness of breath and accessory muscle use" fits the safer response to breathing problems outrank routine requests.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "A patient asking for a TV remote" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: "A patient waiting for routine teaching" misses the bedside risk because breathing difficulty with accessory muscle use is an immediate assessment priority points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue" moves care toward routine observation before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.c: Because severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency suggests clinical safety risk, "The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: Because severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency suggests clinical safety risk, "These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before renal protection.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave renal injury untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids" fits the safer response to a client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg.
distractorRationales.d: "Teach the client about low-sodium foods" uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. The cue "The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before teaching or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: "Obtain a full allergy history before intervening" misses the bedside risk because the causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: "Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: "Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention" fits the safer response to a client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure.
distractorRationales.d: "Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes" misses the bedside risk because severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline" fits the safer response to a nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia.
distractorRationales.c: "Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline" fits the safer response to chills, low back pain.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: "Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "c diff spore precautions infection" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Place the client on droplet precautions only" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.d: Because c diff spore precautions infection suggests infection progression, "Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: Because consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections suggests infection progression, "Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.
